code: library(grf) library(UtopiaPlanitia)  set.seed(1995) n <- 500; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", 1:p) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n)  cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W)  # S3 summary: ATE, variable importance, heterogeneity tests summary(cf)  # S3 plot: diagnostics (default), or type = "rank", "pdp", "inter" plot(cf, type = "rank")  # LOCO variable importance vi <- cf_loco(cf) summary(vi) plot(vi)

title: Auto-Selected Nuisance Causal Forest — autocf
text: function . diagnostic + drop-replacement. grf's default nuisance estimators (regression forests Y W) usually fine always best. autocf() runs small candidate pool shared K-fold cross-fit protocol weighted CV loss (mean-squared error Y continuous W; Brier score binary W, identical MSE 0/1 outcomes), reports comparison table, swaps competitor beats grf baseline margin choose. Apples--apples grf baseline. "grf" candidate pool scored using c.forest$Y.hat / W.hat, come grf's internal honest-split subsampling protocol — strict K-fold cross-fitting — unfair compare true K-fold OOF predictions candidates. Instead, autocf() re-trains grf::regression_forest held-fold's training set predicts held-fold, producing strict K-fold OOF predictions "grf" candidate identically every candidate. 1-SE rule swap. finite-sample CV noise non-trivial candidate produce random improvement particular seed. Default min_improvement = "1se" requires mean fold-wise loss reduction exceed paired-fold standard error reduction: $$\text{swap} \iff   \bar d_K - \widehat{SE}(\bar d_K) > 0,   \quad d_k = \text{loss}^{\text{grf}}_k - \text{loss}^{\text{cand}}_k.$$ kind conservativism glmnet's lambda.1se uses, generalized model selection. Use min_improvement = "0" force improvement fire, supply numeric absolute threshold. Weighted CV losses. c.forest$sample.weights non-trivial, training per-fold loss computation weighted, loss minimised matches chosen nuisances actually used downstream grf. Candidates accept training weights (currently tabpfn) dropped pool warning weights non-trivial. Selection-induced inference. Picking best M candidates CV loss model-selection step. benign grf::causal_forest() uses Neyman-orthogonal R-learner score second stage; small perturbations chosen nuisance invalidate sqrt(n) inference (Chernozhukov et al., 2018; Foster & Syrgkanis, 2019). Without orthogonality safe. Convergence-rate caveat. CV-loss minimization picks lowest finite-sample loss, best convergence rate. valid sqrt(n) downstream inference (e.g. grf::average_treatment_effect()), chosen nuisances must converge o(n^{-1/4}). default pool members (grf reg-forest, glmnet sparsity, xgboost boosting consistency, BART Rockova & van der Pas, 2020) believed adequate typical n; tabpfn formal rate proof treated exploratory downstream confidence intervals matter. Reproducibility future plans. xgboost's mlr3 AutoTuner inner CV runs future framework. prevent non-deterministic seeding user-set future::plan(multisession) similar, autocf() forces future::plan("sequential") duration call restores previous plan exit.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 400; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 200)  # Default: compare grf, glmnet, xgboost, tabpfn, bart (whatever is installed) cf2 <- autocf(cf, K = 5) attr(cf2, "autocf_meta")$scores attr(cf2, "autocf_meta")$winner_y attr(cf2, "autocf_meta")$winner_w  # Smaller pool, force any improvement to swap cf3 <- autocf(cf, pool = c("grf", "glmnet"), min_improvement = "0") } # }

title: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
text: LOCO measures. Leave-one-covariate-(LOCO) importance drops covariate (group covariates) model, refits forest without , measures much worse individualized predictions become. large score means variable important predicting heterogeneous treatment effects; score near zero means forest can recover CATEs without variable. Debiasing. Naive LOCO biased refitting forest introduces retrain variance. implementation uses debiased estimator Benard Josse (2023): full-data refit used bias correction term, per-observation CATE re-estimation performed via forest weights rather raw OOB predictions. Computational cost. function refits p + 1 causal forests (one per dropped variable plus one full-data refit), hyperparameters original. large datasets many covariates, consider using screen argument reduce number refits. Normalization. normalize = TRUE, negative importance scores (can occur dropping variable improves predictions) set zero, remaining scores rescaled sum one. gives proportional importance interpretation discards directional information. Variable grouping. Highly correlated covariates can grouped dropped together. Use group..corr = TRUE automatic grouping via correlation threshold, supply manual list via variable.groups.

code: # \donttest{ library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) vi <- cf_loco(cf) summary(vi) #> LOCO Variable Importance (Benard and Josse, 2023) #>   n = 200  p = 5  #>   Normalized: FALSE  #>  #>  Variable Importance #>        X1   0.976803 #>        X2  -0.008422 #>        X3  -0.008641 #>        X5  -0.012525 #>        X4  -0.013253 plot(vi)   # Normalized importance (sums to 1) vi_norm <- cf_loco(cf, normalize = TRUE) summary(vi_norm) #> LOCO Variable Importance (Benard and Josse, 2023) #>   n = 200  p = 5  #>   Normalized: TRUE  #>  #>  Variable Importance #>        X1          1 #>        X2          0 #>        X3          0 #>        X4          0 #>        X5          0 # }

title: Causal Forest with cv.glmnet-Estimated Nuisances — glmcf
text: changes vs. grf::causal_forest()? nuisance predictions used centre Y W. CATE estimator, honesty splits, identification assumptions inherited grf. cv.glmnet? Penalized regression fast, well-understood baseline nuisance estimation Robinson-style residualisation (Chernozhukov et al., 2018, treat lasso/elastic-net nuisances canonical DML first stage). Linearity--X assumption (load-bearing). glmcf() fits cv.glmnet X supplied, Y.hat W.hat linear columns X (elastic-net penalty + optional relax). true m(x) = E[Y | X] e(x) = E[W | X] non-linear X, lasso residuals carry residual confounding correlated X, biases Robinson centering propagates CATE estimates downstream. grf's residual--residual splits absorb . non-linear nuisances, pre-transform X (interactions, splines, polynomials) passing glmcf(), use tabcf() handles non-linearity natively. DML rate requirement downstream inference. valid sqrt(n) inference grf::average_treatment_effect() (similar), DML theory requires nuisances converge o(n^{-1/4}). Lasso achieves s_n * sqrt(log(p) / n) approximate sparsity (effective sparsity s_n, ambient dimension p). small p sparse signal satisfied; moderate p (e.g. p > sqrt(n)) non-sparse signal , ATE confidence intervals grf -cover. wide-X / non-sparse regime, prefer tabcf() random-forest nuisances. Cross-fitting. Predictions produced via K-fold cross-fitting: fold, cv.glmnet() fit K - 1 folds predicts held-fold. c.forest$clusters non-trivial, folds built units cluster end fold (preserving grf's cluster-aware inference). Treatment type. W treated binary {0, 1}-valued (numeric integer levels present), logical, 2-level factor; case family = "binomial" used predictions clipped [eps, 1 - eps] preserve overlap. Otherwise W treated continuous family = "gaussian" used. W numeric exactly 2 unique values {0, 1} (e.g. {1, 2} {-1, 1}), glmcf() emits warning falls back gaussian regressor; encode treatments 0/1 use classifier. Alpha tuning reproducibility. Within cross-fit fold, inner CV run single shared foldid across candidate alphas minimum-CV-deviance comparison across alphas splits. selected alpha may differ across folds Y W. Per-fold reselection consistent rate data-dependent: small n_train, differences CV deviance across alphas within sampling error, selected alpha can vary resampling (actual selections recorded attr(, "glmcf_meta")$alpha_y $alpha_w diagnostics). studies need reproducible CIs resampling (bootstrap, Monte Carlo), prefer single fixed alpha (e.g. alpha = 1 pure lasso) default grid. Sample weights. c.forest$sample.weights passed cv.glmnet() via weights argument weighted training consistent nuisance fits downstream causal forest. Dependencies. glmnet package gated Suggests dependency. glmcf() calls rlang::check_installed("glmnet") top raises informative error missing.

what: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: Computes leave-one-covariate-(LOCO) variable importance ranger random forests. Two modes available: split-sample LOCO conformal inference (confidence intervals p-values), OOB-based LOCO (point estimates ).

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
code: loco(model, alpha = 0.1, split = TRUE, seed = 1995, verbose = FALSE)

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: model fitted ranger object (must fit keep.inbag = TRUE using OOB mode, training data must recoverable call). alpha Significance level conformal inference intervals. Default 0.1 (90% intervals). used split = TRUE. split Logical. TRUE (default), uses split-sample LOCO via conformalInference::loco(). FALSE, uses OOB prediction error differences. seed Integer seed reproducibility. Default 1995. verbose Logical. Print progress conformal inference? Default FALSE. used split = TRUE.

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: data frame sorted descending importance columns: variable Covariate name. importance LOCO importance score (midpoint CI split = TRUE, increase OOB error split = FALSE). ci.lower Lower confidence bound (split = TRUE). ci.upper Upper confidence bound (split = TRUE). p.value P-value conformal Z-test (split = TRUE).

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: Choosing mode. split-sample method (split = TRUE) provides valid confidence intervals p-values via conformal inference, slower splits data internally. OOB method (split = FALSE) faster suitable screening exploratory analysis, provides point estimates formal inference. Split-sample mode requires conformalInference package, available GitHub:   OOB mode refits original ranger model per covariate, time dropping one predictor, compares OOB prediction error full-model baseline. importance score increase OOB error variable removed; larger values indicate important variables.
code: devtools::install_github("ryantibs/conformal", subdir = "conformalInference")

text: function combines five tests treatment effect heterogeneity, grouped Preferred (valid size) Heuristic categories: Preferred tests (valid Type error control): Sequential RATE (Wager, 2024): k-fold cross-validated test correct size (valid Type error). trustworthy test battery preferred formal inference. Calibration test (Chernozhukov et al., 2018): Regresses doubly robust scores forest's CATE predictions. "differential forest prediction" coefficient tests whether forest captures meaningful heterogeneity beyond ATE. Heuristic tests (useful screening, formal inference): High vs. low CATE, cross-fit (Athey Wager, 2019): Splits sample half, predicts CATEs half using forest trained half, median-splits compares \(ATE_{high}\) vs. \(ATE_{low}\) within held-half. Cross-fitting avoids winner's curse contaminates naive -data version (see grf PR #1502). OOB RATE, two-sided (heuristic): Uses --bag CATE predictions directly. Known anti-conservative (~30\ null nominal 5\ screening tool, formal inference. OOB RATE, one-sided (heuristic): One-sided version . Approximately valid direction heterogeneity pre-specified.

text: BLP panel renders calibration regression $$\psi_i = \beta_1 \bar\tau + \beta_2 (\hat\tau_i - \bar\tau) + \epsilon_i$$ (\(\psi_i\) = AIPW score; \(\hat\tau_i\) = OOB CATE) partial-residual scatter \(\beta_2\). OOB TOC panel draws TOC(q) = ATE among top-q units ranked predicted CATE, minus overall ATE, 95% pointwise band reports AUTOC (QINI) summary grf::rank_average_treatment_effect().

title: Causal Forest with TabPFN-Estimated Nuisances — tabcf
text: changes vs. grf::causal_forest()? nuisance predictions used centre Y W. CATE estimator, honesty splits, identification assumptions inherited grf. TabPFN? TabPFN transformer pre-trained synthetic tabular generative processes frequently outperforms gradient boosting small--medium tabular regression binary classification tasks. Better nuisance fits reduce bias Robinson (1988) residualisation step underlies grf::causal_forest(). Cross-fitting. TabPFN nuisance predictions produced via K-fold cross-fitting: fold, TabPFN trained K - 1 folds predicts held-fold. avoids using unit's outcome predict residual. c.forest$clusters non-trivial, folds built units cluster end fold (preserving grf's cluster-aware inference). Treatment type. W treated binary {0, 1}-valued (numeric integer levels present), logical, 2-level factor; case TabPFN's classifier fit propensity scores clipped [eps, 1 - eps] preserve overlap. Otherwise W treated continuous TabPFN's regressor fit. W numeric exactly 2 unique values {0, 1} (e.g. {1, 2} {-1, 1}), tabcf() emits warning falls back regressor; encode treatments 0/1 use classifier. Tuning. tuning argument controls grf::causal_forest() tunable hyperparameters (min.node.size, mtry, sample.fraction, honesty.fraction, honesty.prune.leaves, alpha, imbalance.penalty) handled refit: "orig" (default): inherit verbatim c.forest$tunable.params. preserves whatever grf chose (user supplied) original forest. re-tuning. "cf.default": omit keys entirely grf::causal_forest() falls back hardcoded defaults. Use original forest's tuning idiosyncratic want clean baseline. re-tuning. "cf.autotune": omit inherited keys set tune.parameters = "" grf::causal_forest() re-tunes scratch new TabPFN nuisances. expensive option one lets tuner respond new nuisance fit. Pass tune.num.trees, tune.num.reps, tune.num.draws via ... need non-default tuner controls. three modes, anything caller passes via ... overrides value chosen tuning. TabPFN seed passthrough. Reproducibility TabPFN governed random_state inside control argument tabpfn::tab_pfn(). tabpfn_args already contain control element, tabcf() builds one per fold via tabpfn::control_tab_pfn(random_state = seed + k) fold gets deterministic distinct TabPFN seed derived user-provided seed. user supplies tabpfn_args$control, control object passed verbatim every fold (user responsible seeding). Dependencies TabPFN backend. tabpfn R package (tidymodels-style wrapper around Python TabPFN backend, v0.1.0+) gated Suggests dependency. tabcf() calls rlang::check_installed("tabpfn") top raises informative error missing. Python backend additionally requires one-time license acceptance: register https://ux.priorlabs.ai, accept license, copy API key, set TABPFN_TOKEN environment calling tabcf(). Without , underlying Python call raises TabPFNLicenseError. easiest way set token inside R call setup_tabpfn_token(), checks variable, walks registering https://ux.priorlabs.ai/account, optionally appends TABPFN_TOKEN=... ~/.Renviron future sessions pick automatically. tabcf() calls setup_tabpfn_token() variable missing interactive session.

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf  <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 200)  # default: inherit grf's tuned params from cf cf2 <- tabcf(cf, K = 5)  # use grf's hardcoded defaults cf3 <- tabcf(cf, K = 5, tuning = "cf.default")  # re-tune from scratch under TabPFN nuisances cf4 <- tabcf(cf, K = 5, tuning = "cf.autotune") } # }
